title: Dually tunable YBCO coplanar waveguide resonators based on helium-ion-generated Josephson inductances
authors: Kenny Fohmann, Timo J. Glebe-Märklin, Benedikt Wilde, Mohamad Kazouini, Christoph Schmid, Dieter Koelle, Reinhold Kleiner, Daniel Bothner
abstract: Superconducting microwave circuits with and without Josephson inductances are the Swiss Army knife for many experiments and technologies from quantum information science to astrophysical particle detectors. Despite a large variety of existing circuit types, thin film materials and Josephson junction technologies, a flexible and reliable platform for high-magnetic-field and high-temperature applications is yet to be found. In this manuscript, we investigate coplanar waveguide cavities made of the high-temperature cuprate superconductor YBa$_2$Cu$_3$O$_7$ (YBCO), integrated with Josephson inductances and quantum interferometers that are generated by the controlled local irradiation of the YBCO with a focused helium ion beam. We obtain strongly flux-tunable microwave resonators, which not only display periodic interferometer oscillations of resonance frequency and decay rate, but also a superimposed Fraunhofer-like modulation pattern. The latter originates from the magnetic field tuning of the individual Josephson junction critical currents due to the out-of-plane junction barriers. It allows adjusting resonance frequency and flux responsivity independently of each other, potentially enabling tunable microwave circuits with low sensitivity to external magnetic-field noise over a broad range of frequencies. Finally, we investigate the temperature dependence of the cavities, show that they have promising characteristics up to 14$\,$K, and present a model for the junction-induced cavity losses.
summary: This paper reports on the creation and characterization of highly tunable superconducting microwave resonators using YBCO thin films and Josephson junctions formed by helium ion irradiation. The key achievement is demonstrating that the resonators exhibit both periodic SQUID oscillations and a superimposed Fraunhofer-like modulation, allowing for independent control over resonance frequency and magnetic flux responsivity. This paves the way for advanced quantum sensing and circuit applications at elevated temperatures.
main_problem: The primary goal is to develop a flexible and reliable platform for superconducting microwave circuits capable of operating in high-magnetic-field and high-temperature regimes.
main_result: The authors successfully demonstrated strongly flux-tunable microwave resonators using YBCO coplanar waveguides integrated with helium-ion-generated Josephson junctions, exhibiting Fraunhofer-like modulation.
method: The study involves fabricating YBCO CPW cavities, generating Josephson junctions via focused helium ion beam irradiation, and characterizing the resulting SQUID-resonator system using a Vector Network Analyzer (VNA) under applied magnetic flux.
model_or_system: The physical system is a coplanar waveguide resonator made of YBCO, incorporating Josephson junctions (bJJs) forming SQUID loops. The analysis models the resonance frequency ($\omega_0$) and losses ($1/Q_{J0}$) as functions of the applied magnetic flux ($\Phi_b$) and temperature ($T_s$).
key_observables: Resonance frequency ($\omega_0$), linewidths ($\kappa$), critical junction current ($I_c$), and the Fraunhofer-like modulation envelope.
important_parameters: Operating temperatures up to 14 K; flux quantum ($\Phi_0$); ion dose ($D_{\ell}$); and the junction resistance ($R_J$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on fitting the data to established SQUID and Josephson junction models, though limitations regarding the simple model for $1/Q_{J0}$ are noted.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate the SQUID flux-tuning curves showing periodic oscillations, overlaid with a broader Fraunhofer-like modulation envelope, and also show the temperature dependence of these characteristics.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from initial characterization of the YBCO platform and junction formation (dose dependence) to measuring the fundamental SQUID flux-tuning response, and finally analyzing the complex interplay between the periodic SQUID modulation and the superimposed Fraunhofer envelope, including temperature studies.
why_interesting: The work directly addresses superconducting circuits using high-temperature superconductors (YBCO), which are central to the study of strongly correlated electron systems, and integrates quantum circuit elements (SQUIDs) into a measurable microwave platform.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Fig. 1: YBCO coplanar waveguide cavities for integration with helium-ion-generated Josephson inductances. (a) Schematic of a quarter-wave coplanar waveguide (CPW) cavity as used in this work (not to scale), capacitively side-coupled to a CPW transmission line. The substrate material is MgO, the superconducting film is 50 nm thick M-type YBCO. For capacitive coupling to the feedline, the cavity has a coupling elbow near its open end. Close to its shorted end far from the feedline, the cavity contains a loop in the CPW center conductor for the SQUID. Characterization is performed by measurements of the scattering matrix element 𝑆21 = 𝑆out/𝑆in. (b) False-color optical microscopy image around...

Fig. 2: Transport characteristics and critical-current ion-dose dependence of M-type YBCO He-bJJs. (a) IVC of a He-FIB bJJ with an ion line dose of 𝐷𝓁= 1000 ions/nm. Critical current is 𝐼c ≈9.5 µA. Top-left inset shows a schematic of the four-terminal IV measurement. Bottom-right inset shows a false-color optical image of the 𝑎dc = 2.5 µm wide bridge structures used for these experi- ments, depicting one set of three parallel bridges that are connected to a common ground but biased and measured individually. (b) Crit- ical junction current 𝐼c vs applied magnetic field 𝐵ext for the junction of panel (a). Inset schematically shows the application of 𝐵ext per- pendicular to the chip surface,...

Fig. 3: Flux-tuning of a helium-ion-generated YBCO SQUID-cavity and parameters of the integrated Josephson junctions. (a) Transmission |𝑆21| around the resonance frequency of R1 without (light blue) and with (purple, pink, red, orange) He-bJJs for different values of bias flux Φb through the SQUID. Introducing the junctions shifts the resonance frequency from 𝜔c = 2𝜋×4.869 GHz to 𝜔0 = 2𝜋×4.705 GHz, mainly due to the additional inductance of the JJs. When a magnetic field perpendicular to the chip surface is applied, equivalent to a bias magnetic flux Φb through the SQUID, the resonance dip shifts further to lower values, here down to 𝜔0 = 2𝜋× 4.39 GHz at Φb/Φ0 ∼0.48. Symbols are data, black...

Fig. 4: Single-bJJ Fraunhofer-diffraction decouples resonance frequency from flux responsivity and allows modeling losses. (a) Gray-scale-coded transmission |𝑆21| vs VNA probe frequency (covering the resonance frequencies of all three cavities) and vs bias flux coupling into the SQUID of R1. Beyond a few flux quanta Φ0, all three modes show a Fraunhofer-like modulation of their resonance frequencies enveloping the periodic modulations of the SQUIDs, which are still clearly visible over the complete field range, even around the Fraunhofer minima or at the first side-maxima, cf. the three zoom windows. Note here that the zoom window for R2 reveals skewed flux arcs, a signature for Josephson...

Fig. 5: Operating flux-tunable YBCO SQUID cavities between 2.1 K and 14 K. (a) Flux-tuning arcs of the resonance frequency 𝜔0 of R1 at three different sample temperatures 𝑇s = 2.1 K, 10 K and 14 K, plotted in purple, red, and orange, respectively. With increasing temperature, the sweetspot resonance frequency decreases, and the tuning range increases from 𝜔00 −𝜔min ∼2𝜋× 240 MHz at 2.1 K to ∼2𝜋× 320 MHz at 14 K. Circles are experimental data, lines are fit curves considering potentially different critical currents of the two bJJs in the SQUID, i.e., an asymmetric SQUID. (b) Critical currents 𝐼1, 𝐼2 of the two bJJs in the SQUID vs 𝑇s obtained from the arc fits in panel (a). Top-filled symbols...
